paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Shaoyu, saying: 'I have heard that when the hundred diseases first begin, they must arise from wind, rain, cold, and heat, following the fine body hair and entering the interstices of the skin. Sometimes the pathogen turns back and departs; sometimes it lingers and lodges; sometimes it becomes wind-swelling with sweating; sometimes it becomes wasting-thirst; sometimes it becomes cold and heat; sometimes it becomes lingering impediment; sometimes it becomes accumulations and gatherings. Strange evils overflow in countless variety, and I wish to hear the reason for this. Now when people fall ill at the same time, some contract this disease and others that — is it perhaps that Heaven generates wind differently for different people? Why should there be such difference?' Shaoyu said: 'Now when Heaven generates wind, it is not out of partiality toward the common people. Its movement is fair, level, and upright: those who transgress against it are struck by it, and those who avoid it escape harm. It is not that Heaven seeks out a person, but that the person transgresses against it of their own accord.'

Huangdi said: 'When people meet with wind at one and the same time and fall ill together, why do their illnesses differ? I wish to hear the reason.' Shaoyu said: 'An excellent question! Let me explain it by comparison with a craftsman. When a craftsman sharpens his axe and hones his blade to cut and fell timber, the wood itself — being of yin and yang — still has its hard and its brittle portions. Where the wood is hard, the blade will not bite; where it is brittle, the bark gives way easily; and where the grain crosses at a knot, the axe itself may be nicked. Now within a single tree, the hard and the brittle portions differ: the hard is unyielding, the brittle is easily damaged — how much more so, then, when the timbers themselves differ, in the thickness of their bark and the abundance of their sap, so that each is distinct from the others! Consider trees that flower early and put forth leaves before others: if they meet with spring frost and fierce wind, their blossoms fall and their leaves wither. In prolonged exposure and great drought, those trees that are brittle-wooded and thin-barked have branches and twigs with little sap, and their leaves wither. In prolonged overcast and excessive rain, those that are thin-barked and rich in sap have their bark soaked through and shallowly rotted. When sudden violent wind arises, trees that are hard yet brittle have their branches snapped and broken. When autumn frost comes with swift wind, trees that are hard yet brittle have their roots shaken and their leaves shed. In each of these five cases, the harm done differs according to the tree — how much more must this be so for human beings!'

Huangdi said: 'How does this correspond in human beings, as with trees?' Shaoyu answered: 'Where a tree is harmed, it is always the branches that suffer the harm; a branch that is hard, brittle yet firm does not yet come to harm. In human beings, when a person has a recurring illness, it is likewise because of some place — in the bones and joints, the skin, or the interstices — that is not firm and solid; it is there that the pathogen lodges, and so illness recurs there constantly.'

Huangdi said: 'In people who are prone to wind reversal with dripping sweat, how may this be known in advance?' Shaoyu answered: 'When the interior is not firm and the interstices are loose, one is prone to wind disease.' Huangdi said: 'How may one know that the flesh is not firm?' Shaoyu answered: 'When the flesh of the hollow of the knee is not firm and lacks distinct texture — that is, when its texture is coarse, and where the texture is coarse the skin is not close-knit — the interstices are loose. This describes the condition taken as a whole.'

Huangdi said: 'In people who are prone to wasting-thirst, how may this be known in advance?' Shaoyu answered: 'Those in whom all five viscera are soft and weak are prone to wasting-thirst.' Huangdi said: 'How does one recognize that the five viscera are soft and weak?' Shaoyu answered: 'Now where there is softness and weakness, there must also be hardness and strength present together with it; hardness and strength incline toward frequent anger, while the soft is easily injured.' Huangdi said: 'How does one distinguish the soft and weak from the hard and strong?' Shaoyu answered: 'Such a person has thin skin, and eyes that are firm, fixed, and deep-set; the passage from throat to intestine is long and straight. Their heart is hard, and hardness inclines toward frequent anger; anger causes the qi to rebel upward, accumulating and gathering within the chest, so that blood and qi flow backward and lodge there. The flesh beneath the flank becomes engorged, the blood vessels fail to circulate, and this turns into heat; heat then consumes the flesh and skin, and so wasting-thirst results. This describes a person who is fierce and hard by temperament but whose flesh and muscles are weak.'

Huangdi said: 'In people who are prone to cold-heat disorders, how may this be known in advance?' Shaoyu answered: 'Those with small bones and weak flesh are prone to cold-heat disorders.' Huangdi said: 'How does one determine whether the bones are large or small, the flesh hard or brittle, and the complexion uneven?' Shaoyu answered: 'The cheekbone is the root of the bony structure: when the cheekbone is large, the bones are large; when the cheekbone is small, the bones are small. When the skin is thin and the flesh lacks firm bulk, when the arms are soft and feeble, and when the complexion at the lower portion of the face appears dull and lifeless, not matching the color of the upper portion, but sullied and distinctly different — this is the sign. Moreover, in one whose arms are thin, the marrow is not full, and so such a person is prone to cold-heat disorders.'

Huangdi said: 'How may one recognize a person prone to impediment disorders?' Shaoyu answered: 'One whose skin texture is coarse and whose flesh is not firm is prone to impediment.' Huangdi said: 'Does impediment have fixed locations, whether high or low in the body?' Shaoyu answered: 'To know whether it will occur high or low, one need only observe the region affected in each case.'

Huangdi said: 'In people prone to accumulations and gatherings within the intestines, how may this be known in advance?' Shaoyu answered: 'When the skin is thin and lacks luster, and the flesh is not firm but moist and soft, then the intestines and stomach are in poor condition. Being in poor condition, evil qi lingers and lodges there; accumulations and gatherings then injure the region between the spleen and stomach. When cold and warmth are not properly regulated, evil qi gradually arrives, accumulating and lingering until it lodges there, and a great gathering then arises.'

Huangdi said: 'I have now heard of the forms these illnesses take, and I understand them. I wish now to hear of their timing.' Shaoyu answered: 'One must first establish the person's age, so as to know the proper season. If the season is in its ascendant phase, the illness will arise; if the season is in its declining phase, the danger is greater still. Even where there is no marked decline, if in that year there is a pathway of clash and encounter, the illness will certainly arise. This is what is meant by disease arising according to the body's constitution — this is the guiding thread of the five variations.'

title_en: Discourse on the Root Nature of the Viscera
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo, saying: 'The blood, qi, essence, and spirit of a person are what sustain life and circulate throughout one's nature and destiny. The channels are what move the blood and qi, nourish yin and yang, moisten the sinews and bones, and benefit the joints. The defensive qi is what warms the flesh between the muscles, fills out the skin, makes the interstices firm and full, and governs their opening and closing. The will and intent are what govern the essence and spirit, gather in the ethereal and corporeal souls, adapt to cold and warmth, and harmonize joy and anger. Therefore, when the blood is harmonious, the channels flow freely, nutritive qi restores yin and yang in proper balance, the sinews and bones are strong and vigorous, and the joints move clearly and smoothly. When the defensive qi is harmonious, the flesh between the muscles is loose and free, the skin is well-regulated and supple, and the interstices are close and dense. When the will and intent are harmonious, the essence and spirit are focused and upright, the ethereal and corporeal souls do not scatter, regret and anger do not arise, and the five viscera do not receive evil qi. When cold and warmth are harmonious, the six bowels transform the grain properly, wind impediment does not arise, the channels flow freely and smoothly, and the limbs and joints find peace. This is the ordinary state of balance in a person. The five viscera are what store the essence, spirit, blood, qi, and the ethereal and corporeal souls. The six bowels are what transform water and grain and move the body fluids along. These are the endowments that every person alike receives from Heaven, whether foolish or wise, worthy or unworthy, with no difference to favor one over another. And yet there are those who alone live out their full heavenly span, free of evil and deviant illness, undiminished after a hundred years, so that even when they encounter wind and rain, sudden cold, or extreme heat, none of these are able to harm them; while there are others who never leave the shelter and shade of their own rooms, free from any apprehension or fright, and yet still cannot escape illness. Why is this? I wish to hear the reason.'

Qibo replied: 'This is indeed a searching question. The five viscera are what participate with Heaven and Earth, correspond to yin and yang, set the four seasons in motion, and transform the five periods. The five viscera themselves inherently differ in being small or large, high or low in position, hard or brittle, upright or tilted; and the six bowels likewise differ in being small or large, long or short, thick or thin, coiled or straight, slack or tense. In all, these are twenty-five distinct variations, each different from the others — some favorable, some unfavorable, some auspicious, some inauspicious. Allow me to explain their particulars.'

When the heart is small, it is at ease, and evil qi cannot injure it, though it is easily injured by worry. When the heart is large, worry cannot injure it, but it is easily injured by evil qi. When the heart is positioned high, it presses full against the lung, causing oppression and forgetfulness, and such a person is difficult to move with words. When the heart is positioned low, the viscus lies exposed toward the exterior, easily injured by cold, and such a person is easily frightened by words. When the heart is firm, the viscus is secure and well-guarded. When the heart is brittle, one is prone to wasting-thirst and heat in the middle. When the heart is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the heart is tilted to one side, one's conduct lacks consistency, with no proper self-governance.

When the lung is small, one drinks little and does not suffer panting and wheezing. When the lung is large, one drinks much, and is prone to chest impediment, throat impediment, and rebellious qi. When the lung is positioned high, qi rises upward, causing labored breathing with the shoulders heaving and coughing. When the lung is positioned low, it presses down against the diaphragm and crowds the lung itself, and one is prone to pain beneath the rib-side. When the lung is firm, one does not suffer illness — apart from cough with rising qi. When the lung is brittle, one suffers from wasting-thirst and impediment and is easily injured. When the lung is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the lung is tilted to one side, one suffers pain on one side of the chest.

When the liver is small, the viscus is secure, and there is no disease beneath the rib-side. When the liver is large, it presses upon the stomach and crowds against the throat; crowding the throat causes distress within the diaphragm, together with pain beneath the rib-side. When the liver is positioned high, it presses upward and cuts against the diaphragm, and the rib-side is drawn taut, producing 'panting mass.' When the liver is positioned low, it presses upon the stomach, leaving the region beneath the rib-side empty and hollow; when this region is hollow, it is easily invaded by evil qi. When the liver is firm, the viscus is secure and difficult to injure. When the liver is brittle, one is prone to wasting-thirst and impediment, and is easily injured. When the liver is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the liver is tilted to one side, one suffers pain beneath the rib-side.

When the spleen is small, the viscus is secure and difficult to injure with evil qi. When the spleen is large, one suffers pain from crowding at the soft flank, and cannot walk quickly. When the spleen is positioned high, the soft flank draws upon the free ribs and causes pain. When the spleen is positioned low, it descends and presses down toward the large intestine; pressing down upon the large intestine, the viscus readily receives evil qi. When the spleen is firm, the viscus is secure and difficult to injure. When the spleen is brittle, one is prone to wasting-thirst and impediment, and is easily injured. When the spleen is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the spleen is tilted to one side, one is prone to fullness and distension.

When the kidney is small, the viscus is secure and difficult to injure. When the kidney is large, one is prone to lower back pain, unable to bend forward or backward, and easily injured by evil qi. When the kidney is positioned high, one suffers pain along the back and spine, unable to bend forward or backward. When the kidney is positioned low, one suffers pain in the lower back and sacrum, unable to bend forward or backward, and develops fox-mounting hernia. When the kidney is firm, one does not suffer pain in the lower back. When the kidney is brittle, one is prone to wasting-thirst, and is easily injured. When the kidney is upright and centered, it is harmonious and smooth, and difficult to injure. When the kidney is tilted to one side, one suffers pain in the lower back and sacrum. In all, these twenty-five variations are the recurring afflictions that trouble people.

Huangdi said: 'How can one know this?' Qibo said: 'One whose complexion is red and whose skin texture is fine has a small heart; one whose texture is coarse has a large heart. One who lacks a xiphoid process has a heart positioned high; one whose xiphoid process is small, short, and raised has a heart positioned low. One whose xiphoid process is long has a firm heart; one whose xiphoid process is weak, small, and thin has a brittle heart. One whose xiphoid process extends straight down without being raised has an upright, centered heart; one whose xiphoid process leans to one side has a heart tilted to one side.'

One whose complexion is white and whose skin texture is fine has a small lung; one whose texture is coarse has a large lung. One with broad shoulders, a projecting chest, and a sunken throat has a lung positioned high; one whose armpits are drawn close and whose rib-sides are spread wide has a lung positioned low. One with well-formed shoulders and back, thick and full, has a firm lung; one whose shoulders and back are thin has a brittle lung. One whose back and chest are thick has an upright, centered lung; one whose rib-sides are uneven and loosely formed has a lung tilted to one side.

One whose complexion is green-blue and whose skin texture is fine has a small liver; one whose texture is coarse has a large liver. One with a broad chest and flaring lower ribs has a liver positioned high; one whose rib-sides are drawn close with narrow, rabbit-like lower ribs has a liver positioned low. One whose chest and rib-sides are well-formed has a firm liver; one whose rib bones are weak has a brittle liver. One whose chest and abdomen are well-proportioned and matched has an upright, centered liver; one whose rib bones are raised unevenly to one side has a liver tilted to one side.

One whose complexion is yellow and whose skin texture is fine has a small spleen; one whose texture is coarse has a large spleen. One whose lips are raised and everted has a spleen positioned high; one whose lower lip hangs loose has a spleen positioned low. One whose lips are firm has a firm spleen; one whose lips are large but not firm has a brittle spleen. One whose upper and lower lips are well-matched has an upright, centered spleen; one whose lips are raised unevenly to one side has a spleen tilted to one side.

One whose complexion is black and whose skin texture is fine has a small kidney; one whose texture is coarse has a large kidney. One with high-set ears has a kidney positioned high; one whose ears are sunken behind has a kidney positioned low. One whose ears are firm has a firm kidney; one whose ears are thin and not firm has a brittle kidney. One whose ears are well-formed and set forward near the jaw has an upright, centered kidney; one whose ears are set unevenly high has a kidney tilted to one side. In all these various conditions, when the corresponding feature is well-maintained, the person is at ease; when it is diminished or deficient, illness results.

Huangdi said: 'Excellent. But this is not what I originally asked. I wish to hear about those people who cannot be made ill — who live out their full heavenly span, and even when deeply disturbed or greatly frightened, their resolve cannot be diminished, and even severe cold or extreme heat cannot harm them — as well as about those who never leave the shelter of their own rooms and have no apprehension or fright, and yet still cannot escape illness. Why is this? I wish to hear the reason.' Qibo said: 'The five viscera and six bowels are the lodging places of evil qi — allow me to explain the reason. When all five viscera are small, one has little illness but suffers from an anxious, troubled heart and great worry and disturbance. When all five viscera are large, one is slow to attend to affairs and difficult to disturb or trouble. When all five viscera are positioned high, one is inclined to lofty ambition and grand undertakings. When all five viscera are positioned low, one is inclined to place oneself below others. When all five viscera are firm, one has no illness. When all five viscera are brittle, one is never free of illness. When all five viscera are upright and centered, one is harmonious and agreeable and wins the hearts of others. When all five viscera are tilted to one side, one harbors a deceitful heart and is inclined to thieving, cannot deal fairly with others, and is inconsistent and duplicitous in speech.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the correspondences of the six bowels.' Qibo answered: 'The lung is paired with the large intestine, and the large intestine corresponds to the skin. The heart is paired with the small intestine, and the small intestine corresponds to the vessels. The liver is paired with the gallbladder, and the gallbladder corresponds to the sinews. The spleen is paired with the stomach, and the stomach corresponds to the flesh. The kidney is paired with the triple burner and bladder, and the triple burner and bladder correspond to the interstices and fine body hair.'

Huangdi said: 'How does this correspondence work?' Qibo said: 'The lung corresponds to the skin. When the skin is thick, the large intestine is thick; when the skin is thin, the large intestine is thin. When the skin is loose and the abdomen is large within, the large intestine is large and long. When the skin is tight, the large intestine is tight and short. When the skin is smooth, the large intestine is straight. When the skin and flesh do not separate easily from each other, the large intestine is knotted.'

The heart corresponds to the vessels. When the skin is thick, the vessels are thick, and when the vessels are thick, the small intestine is thick. When the skin is thin, the vessels are thin, and when the vessels are thin, the small intestine is thin. When the skin is loose, the vessels are loose, and when the vessels are loose, the small intestine is large and long. When the skin is thin and the pulsing vessels are small, the small intestine is small and short. When the yang channel vessels are all markedly winding and tortuous, the small intestine is knotted.

The spleen corresponds to the flesh. When the muscular flesh is firm and full, the stomach is thick; when the muscular flesh is slight, the stomach is thin. When the muscular flesh is small and slight, the stomach is not firm. When the flesh does not match the body in proportion, the stomach is positioned low, and when the stomach is low, the lower duct is constricted and does not function smoothly. When the flesh is not firm, the stomach is slack. When the muscular flesh lacks small knotted masses within, the stomach is tight. When the muscular flesh has a moderate number of small knotted masses within, the stomach is knotted, and when the stomach is knotted, the upper duct is constricted and does not function smoothly.

The liver corresponds to the nails. When the nails are thick and yellow in color, the gallbladder is thick. When the nails are thin and red in color, the gallbladder is thin. When the nails are firm and green-blue in color, the gallbladder is tight. When the nails are soft and red in color, the gallbladder is slack. When the nails are straight and white in color without curvature, the gallbladder is straight. When the nails are poorly formed, black in color, with many ridges, the gallbladder is knotted.

The kidney corresponds to the bones. When the skin texture is dense and the skin is thick, the triple burner and bladder are thick; when the skin texture is coarse and the skin is thin, the triple burner and bladder are thin. When the interstices are loose, the triple burner and bladder are slack. When the skin is tight and lacks fine body hair, the triple burner and bladder are tight. When the fine body hair is fine yet full, the triple burner and bladder are straight. When the fine body hair is sparse, the triple burner and bladder are knotted.

Huangdi said: 'Thickness and thinness, fine form and poor form, all have their outward manifestations. I wish to hear of the illnesses associated with each.' Qibo answered: 'By observing the outward correspondence, one comes to know the condition of the inner viscus, and thus one knows what illness will occur.'

Leigong asked Huangdi, saying: 'This lowly disciple has received your instruction and gained understanding of the sixty chapters of the Nine Needles; morning and evening I have diligently applied myself to them, until the more recently bound volumes have had their binding cords worn through, and the older bamboo strips have grown soiled with handling. Yet still I recite and chant them without setting them aside, and there remain points whose meaning I have not fully grasped. In the chapter "External Estimation," it speaks of gathering and binding all things together into one — I do not know what this means. Now, that which is great has nothing outside it, and that which is small has nothing within it; the great and the small are without limit, the high and the low are without measure — how, then, can one bind them into one? Among scholars, natural talent and capacity vary, some deep and some shallow; my own understanding is narrow and superficial, unable to grasp what is broad, vast, and profound, though I strive as hard in study as this lowly disciple can. I fear that this teaching will scatter and be lost to later generations, cut off from my own descendants — may I dare ask how it can be bound into a compact and lasting form?' Huangdi said: 'An excellent question indeed. This is a teaching that my former masters restricted, transmitting it only in private, sealed with a covenant of cutting the arm and smearing the blood in oath. If you wish to receive it, why not first observe ritual fasting?'

Leigong bowed twice, rose, and said: 'I ask to receive your instruction on these terms.' He then fasted and kept vigil for three days, and requested, saying: 'May I ask that, this day being one of proper yang brightness, this lowly disciple be permitted to receive the covenant?' Huangdi then entered the fasting chamber together with him, cut his arm, and smeared the blood in oath. Huangdi himself pronounced the invocation, saying: 'On this day of proper yang brightness, we smear blood in oath to transmit these formulas. Should anyone dare to betray these words, may they instead receive calamity in return.' Leigong bowed twice and said: 'This lowly disciple receives it.' Huangdi then grasped his hand with his left hand, and with his right handed him the text, saying: 'Be cautious, be cautious. I shall now explain it to you. In all the principles of needling, the channel vessels are the starting point. One must trace the course along which they flow, and know their proper measures and dimensions. Within, one needles the five viscera; without, one needles the six bowels. One must carefully examine the defensive qi, for it is the mother of the hundred diseases. One regulates deficiency and excess, and only when deficiency and excess are corrected does one stop; one drains the blood in the network vessels, and when the blood is exhausted, there is no danger.'

Leigong said: 'All of this is what this lowly disciple already understands; what I do not yet know is how to bind it into a compact form.' Huangdi said: 'Now to bind a formula compactly is like binding shut a sack: if the sack is full and not bound shut, its contents will leak and spill out; if a formula is complete but not bound into compact form, the spirit will not accompany it.' Leigong said: 'I wish, for the sake of those of lesser talent, that you would bind it compactly before it becomes overfull.' Huangdi said: 'One who knows how to bind it compactly before it becomes overfull may become a skilled practitioner, but cannot thereby become a teacher to all under Heaven.'

Leigong said: 'I wish to hear how one becomes a skilled practitioner.' Huangdi said: 'The cunkou pulse governs the interior, and the renying pulse governs the exterior. The two correspond to each other, moving together and arriving together, like a stretched cord equal in thickness throughout its length. In spring and summer, the renying pulse is slightly larger; in autumn and winter, the cunkou pulse is slightly larger. When this is so, the person is called balanced and healthy.'

When the renying pulse is one time larger than the cunkou pulse, the disease is in the Foot Shaoyang channel; if it is one time larger and also agitated, it is in the Hand Shaoyang channel. When the renying pulse is two times larger, the disease is in the Foot Taiyang channel; if two times larger and agitated, the disease is in the Hand Taiyang channel. When the renying pulse is three times larger, the disease is in the Foot Yangming channel; if three times larger and agitated, the disease is in the Hand Yangming channel. When the pulse is replete, this indicates heat; when deficient, it indicates cold; when taut, it indicates painful impediment; when intermittent, the condition alternates between severe and eased. When replete, one drains it; when deficient, one supplements it; when taut, one needles the flesh between the muscles; when intermittent, one drains the network vessels of blood and also administers medicinal decoctions; when the pulse is sunken and depressed, one applies moxibustion; when neither replete nor deficient, one selects points along the affected channel itself — this is called channel puncture. When the renying pulse is four times larger, both large and rapid, this is called yang overflow; yang overflow becomes exterior blockage, and is fatal, beyond treatment. One must carefully press and examine the root and branch of the pulse, observe whether it is cold or hot, so as to verify the disease of the viscera and bowels.'

When the cunkou pulse is one time larger than the renying pulse, the disease is in the Foot Jueyin channel; if one time larger and agitated, it is in the Hand Xin Zhu (Pericardium) channel. When the cunkou pulse is two times larger, the disease is in the Foot Shaoyin channel; if two times larger and agitated, it is in the Hand Shaoyin channel. When the cunkou pulse is three times larger, the disease is in the Foot Taiyin channel; if three times larger and agitated, it is in the Hand Taiyin channel. When the pulse is replete, this indicates distension and fullness, cold in the middle, and undigested food; when deficient, it indicates heat in the middle, discharge of sloughing stool, shortness of qi, and altered color of the urine; when taut, it indicates painful impediment; when intermittent, the pain alternates between occurring and ceasing. When replete, one drains it; when deficient, one supplements it; when taut, one needles first and then applies moxibustion afterward; when intermittent, one drains the network vessels of blood and afterward regulates the condition; when sunken and depressed, one applies moxibustion alone — for when the pulse is sunken and depressed, the blood within the vessel has become knotted and bound, with stagnant blood lodged within, and the blood is cold, so moxibustion is fitting. When neither replete nor deficient, one selects points along the affected channel itself. When the cunkou pulse is four times larger, this is called inward blockage; inward blockage, being both large and rapid, is fatal, beyond treatment. One must carefully examine whether the root and branch of the pulse are cold or warm, so as to verify the disease of the viscera and bowels.'

Only when one has thoroughly grasped the nutritive qi and the transport points can one go on to transmit the great essentials. The great essentials state: when replete, simply drain it; when deficient, simply supplement it; when taut, apply moxibustion and needling together with medicinal decoctions; when sunken and depressed, simply apply moxibustion; when neither replete nor deficient, select points along the affected channel itself. What is called treating by the channel means administering medicinal decoctions as well as moxibustion and needling. When the pulse is taut and tense, one should guide and stretch the affected area; when the pulse is large yet weak, one should seek calm and quietude, exerting no strenuous effort or labor.'
